topic: Ministry for Culture and Heritage, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025
description: Wednesday, 18 February 2026 | Social Services and Community Committee Ministry for Culture and Heritage, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025 Hearing of Evidence Ministry for Culture and Heritage Leauanae Laulu Mac Leauanae, Secretary & Chief Executive Brendan Gage, Deputy Secretary, Policy, Performance & Insights Dr. Lauren Sweetman, Principal Advisor, Insights Andrei Zubkov, Senior Policy Adviser, Media & Screen Policy
descriptionSummary: Ministry for Culture and Heritage, Long-Term Insights Briefing 2025. Ministry for Culture and Heritage. Leauanae Laulu Mac Leauanae, Secretary & Chief Executive

chunks.text: Morning, welcome.
Yeah,

We coming in bright and early. Um, we've got, uh, roughly, roughly 60 minutes, um, you this morning, maybe slightly less, um, just to give a grab, a long-term insights briefing. So, um, when you're ready, time is awesome. Thank you Chair and the committee members for your time this morning. And today from the ministry. I'm joined with my learned colleagues to have this corridor all around the long-term insights briefing that is before you.

To my right, I had Brendan and Andre, and to my left is Lauren, who's the project lead for Long-Term Insights Briefing. And the, the, the four of us will reflect and provide some insights, uh, in relation to our discussions this morning. Our long-term insights briefing is focused on culture in the digital age.

This is a broad think piece that navigates the profound changes that digital technology brings to our cultural landscape. By 2040, our cultural sectors will experience deep interconnectivity with AI and other emerging technologies, technologies that may be familiar, that we may be familiar with generative AI software, robotics, blockchain technology, or have already evolved and developed some technologies we haven't even conceived of yet, uh, will be changing our sectors.

When we published the first T TIP in 20, in early 2023, generative AI had only just emerged while writing the T tip, it was hard to keep up with the pace of change. Uh, there were so many advancements, like for example, the release of open AI's first agent operator in Jan 2025 in China's first game of soccer using autonomous robots in June of 2025.

Now, if you get a chance, Mr. Chair and committing members have a laugh at that. These advancements will continue to offer opportunities for creating, sharing and pro protecting stories that are unique to our culture and heritage. If we look at the great work that Teq Media, for example, whose pioneering a AI not language work, has helped to advance to real revitalization and preserving oral histories.

Whilst there are great opportunities, there are also significant ethical, legal, cultural and governance questions. For example, the TIB talks about a range of issues relating to online harm, including use of algorithms, synthetic content, synthetic content, deep fakes and misinformation.

We've already recently seen that folks of the face, the fake Facebook posts shared on, um, sharing AI generated images of children after the Mount Landslide as an example. We must address these issues thoughtfully and collectively. The is a base for us to keep a step out of the week till week issues out of the month to month issues, but allow us to think longer term, decades, generations, even.

This is not the current government policy thinking on ai. Instead, it's a snapshot of a collective exploration across our cultural system of what the future may hold. We spoke to a lot of people in writing this out to, and I do want to acknowledge all the funded entities within the vote experts, creative practitioners, and everyone who have been able and who have been able and so generous with their time, experiences, and insights to help us develop the out to, uh, we are a small but lean agency, uh, with a mix of levers

and a sector stewardship approach. I really do want to acknowledge our crowdfunded entities who played an important role in mapping out our future. The methodology was that we, through combination of interviews, workshops, written submissions and conversations engaged with over a hundred people on the, and we saw and heard firsthand how polarizing the issues arising from emerging digital technology can be, particularly in this generative AI space.

We heard the risks and opportunities of emerging technology and the competing priorities across commercial and public spheres. We as decision makers will always have to navigate these tensions and trade offs. This is probably the critical part of the t and allows a framework for us to have these important conversations.

There are many examples around this tension. AI is scraping huge volumes of cultural and creative content, including New Zealand music, images, stories, design media and heritage material to train AI systems. This scraping is often done without permission or compensation.

This is an issue being faced globally. So we are monitoring developments that are happening globally and here at home to consider the implications on our cultural systems. This includes staying in touch with our counterparts at MB who are leading the review of the Copyright Act. While the digital future is an era of interest for everyone, we all have our reckons.

There are such diverse perspectives on how people within the different sectors view emerging technologies. We know that the use of AI assisted tools varies quite a bit between sectors, and this is influenced by many factors including digital access and skills and the nature of the work being undertaken.

And there's a wide, and there is such a wide ranging applications for emerging digital technology in our sectors. From creating and editing audio visual content to transcription and captioning to interactive digital cultural experiences to global distribution.

It is exciting to see how New Zealand creatives and communities are harnessing the new technology to innovate, to preserve our culture and history for future generations. How does the outer impact on the way the ministry thinks and the sectors insights provide an evidence base to support our sectors to be sustainable and resilient and to adapt to the digital future?

The insights within the alt tip align with our government's recent strategy for ai, which emphasizes AI adoption and application with a focus on leveraging AI science, innovation and technology strengths. It also aligns with the government, Digi government chief digital officer's work on digitizing government and responsible AI adoption.

There are examples also under amplify New Zealand's creative and cultural strategy that delve into these issues. We also utilize futures thinking, which is a tool that we will, as a ministry continue to use around our thinking in the cultural sectors. So that's a brief overview, insights into the tib, and we, my colleagues here, look forward to future conversation with you now.
